title: Alma mater

infobox.value: (Latin: *[alma](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/almus#Latin "wikt:almus") [mater](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/mater#Latin "wikt:mater")*; pl.: **almae matres**) is an [allegorical](/wiki/Allegory "Allegory") [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") phrase meaning 'nourishing mother'. It personifies a [school](/wiki/School "School") that a person has attended or graduated from.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) The term is related to *[alumnus](/wiki/Alumnus "Alumnus")*, meaning 'nursling', which describes a school graduate.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

outgoing_links.text: European Academy of Sciences and Arts
outgoing_links.target_title: European Academy of Sciences and Arts
Mary Institute and St. Louis Country Day School

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

School or university that a person has attended or graduated from

For the type of song, see [Alma mater (song)](/wiki/Alma_mater_(song) "Alma mater (song)"). For other uses, see [Alma mater (disambiguation)](/wiki/Alma_mater_(disambiguation) "Alma mater (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Columbia_University%2C_NYC_%28June_2014%29_-_09.JPG/250px-Columbia_University%2C_NYC_%28June_2014%29_-_09.JPG)](/wiki/File:Columbia_University,_NYC_(June_2014)_-_09.JPG)

The *[Alma Mater](/wiki/Alma_Mater_(New_York_sculpture) "Alma Mater (New York sculpture)")* statue at [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), cast by [Daniel Chester French](/wiki/Daniel_Chester_French "Daniel Chester French") in 1903

**Alma mater** (Latin: *[alma](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/almus#Latin "wikt:almus") [mater](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/mater#Latin "wikt:mater")*; pl.: **almae matres**) is an [allegorical](/wiki/Allegory "Allegory") [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") phrase meaning 'nourishing mother'. It personifies a [school](/wiki/School "School") that a person has attended or graduated from.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) The term is related to *[alumnus](/wiki/Alumnus "Alumnus")*, meaning 'nursling', which describes a school graduate.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

In its earliest usage, *alma mater* was an [honorific title](/wiki/Epithet "Epithet") for various [mother goddesses](/wiki/Mother_goddess "Mother goddess"), especially [Ceres](/wiki/Ceres_(mythology) "Ceres (mythology)") or [Cybele](/wiki/Cybele "Cybele").[[5]](#cite_note-OED-5) Later, in [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism"), it became a title for [Mary, mother of Jesus](/wiki/Mary,_mother_of_Jesus "Mary, mother of Jesus"). By the early 17th century, the nursing mother became an allegory for universities. Used by many schools in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") and North America, it has special association with the [University of Bologna](/wiki/University_of_Bologna "University of Bologna"), whose motto *Alma Mater Studiorum* ("nurturing mother of studies") emphasizes its role in [originating the modern university](/wiki/List_of_oldest_universities_in_continuous_operation "List of oldest universities in continuous operation").

Several university campuses in [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") display artistic representations of *alma mater*, depicted as a robed woman wearing a [laurel wreath](/wiki/Laurel_wreath "Laurel wreath") crown. The earliest and most famous of these is the bronze [*Alma Mater* statue](/wiki/Alma_Mater_(New_York_sculpture) "Alma Mater (New York sculpture)") at [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University"), designed in 1901 by [Daniel Chester French](/wiki/Daniel_Chester_French "Daniel Chester French"). In the US the term *alma mater* is often used to describe a school song.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alma_mater&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Legate_John%2C_Alma_Mater_Cantabrigia_Emblem_1600_%28Golden_Chaine_print%29.jpg/250px-Legate_John%2C_Alma_Mater_Cantabrigia_Emblem_1600_%28Golden_Chaine_print%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Legate_John,_Alma_Mater_Cantabrigia_Emblem_1600_(Golden_Chaine_print).jpg)

[John Legate's](/wiki/John_Legate "John Legate") Alma Mater for the [University of Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge"), written in 1600, the first known use of the term

Although *alma* (nourishing) was a common epithet for [Ceres](/wiki/Ceres_(mythology) "Ceres (mythology)"), [Cybele](/wiki/Cybele "Cybele"), [Venus](/wiki/Venus_(mythology) "Venus (mythology)"), and other mother goddesses, it was not frequently used in conjunction with *mater* in classical Latin.[[7]](#cite_note-Sollors-1986-7) In the *[Oxford Latin Dictionary](/wiki/Oxford_Latin_Dictionary "Oxford Latin Dictionary")*, the full phrase's origin is attributed to *[De rerum natura](/wiki/De_rerum_natura "De rerum natura")*, in which Lucretius uses the term as an epithet for an unnamed earth goddess:

Denique caelesti sumus omnes semine oriundi  
omnibus ille idem pater est, unde alma liquentis  
umoris guttas mater cum terra recepit (2.991–993)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

We are all sprung from that celestial seed,  
all of us have same father, from whom earth,  
the nourishing mother, receives drops of liquid moisture

After the [fall of Rome](/wiki/Fall_of_Rome "Fall of Rome"), the term was used in Christian liturgy to describe [Jesus' mother, Mary](/wiki/Mary,_mother_of_Jesus "Mary, mother of Jesus"). "[Alma Redemptoris Mater](/wiki/Alma_Redemptoris_Mater "Alma Redemptoris Mater")" is a well-known eleventh century [antiphon](/wiki/Antiphon "Antiphon") devoted to Mary.[[7]](#cite_note-Sollors-1986-7)

The earliest documented use of the term to refer to a university is in 1600, when the [University of Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge") printer, [John Legate](/wiki/John_Legate "John Legate"), began using an emblem for the [university press](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Press "Cambridge University Press").[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) The first-known appearance of the device is in [William Perkins'](/wiki/William_Perkins_(theologian) "William Perkins (theologian)"), *A Golden Chain*, a book first printed by Legate in 1600. On the title-page, the Latin phrase *Alma Mater Cantabrigia* ("nourishing mother Cambridge") is inscribed on a pedestal bearing a lactating woman wearing a [mural crown](/wiki/Mural_crown "Mural crown").[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

In reference works of English etymology, often the first university-related usage is cited as 1710, when an academic mother figure is mentioned in a remembrance of [Henry More](/wiki/Henry_More "Henry More") by Richard Ward.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Special use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alma_mater&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Special use")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Archiginnasio_ora_blu_Bologna.jpg/250px-Archiginnasio_ora_blu_Bologna.jpg)](/wiki/File:Archiginnasio_ora_blu_Bologna.jpg)

The [University of Bologna](/wiki/University_of_Bologna "University of Bologna"), the [world's oldest university in continuous operation](/wiki/List_of_oldest_universities_in_continuous_operation "List of oldest universities in continuous operation"), known in Latin as *Alma Mater Studiorum* (nourishing mother of studies).

Many historic European universities have adopted *Alma Mater* as part of the Latin translation of their official name. The Latin name of the [University of Bologna](/wiki/University_of_Bologna "University of Bologna"), *Alma Mater Studiorum* (nourishing mother of studies), refers to its status as the [oldest continuously operating university in the world](/wiki/List_of_oldest_universities_in_continuous_operation "List of oldest universities in continuous operation"). At other European universities, such as the [Alma Mater Lipsiensis](/wiki/Leipzig_University "Leipzig University") in Leipzig, Germany, or [Alma Mater Jagiellonica](/wiki/Jagiellonian_University "Jagiellonian University"), Poland, the title emphasizes historic ties to a founding city or dynasty.

The [Alma Mater Europaea](/wiki/Alma_Mater_Europaea "Alma Mater Europaea") in [Salzburg](/wiki/Salzburg "Salzburg"), Austria, an international university founded by the [European Academy of Sciences and Arts](/wiki/European_Academy_of_Sciences_and_Arts "European Academy of Sciences and Arts") in 2010, uses the term as its official name.

In the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), the [College of William & Mary](/wiki/College_of_William_%26_Mary "College of William & Mary") in [Williamsburg, Virginia](/wiki/Williamsburg,_Virginia "Williamsburg, Virginia"), has been called the "Alma Mater of the Nation" because of its ties to the founding of the country.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

At [Queen's University](/wiki/Queen%27s_University_at_Kingston "Queen's University at Kingston") in [Kingston, Ontario](/wiki/Kingston,_Ontario "Kingston, Ontario"), and the [University of British Columbia](/wiki/University_of_British_Columbia "University of British Columbia") in Vancouver, British Columbia, the main student government is known as the Alma Mater Society.

## Monuments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alma_mater&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Monuments")]

Sculptures of *Alma Mater* are found on several North American university campuses. In 1901, to commemorate the opening of the [Low Library](/wiki/Low_Library "Low Library"), [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University") commissioned [Daniel Chester French](/wiki/Daniel_Chester_French "Daniel Chester French") to design a bronze statue of for the library's steps. French chose the *alma mater* to allegorically embody the university without depicting its founder, King George II.[[16]](#cite_note-Holzer-16) A similar sculpture, cast in 1919 by [Mario Korbel](/wiki/Mario_Korbel "Mario Korbel"), sits on the main entrance steps at the [University of Havana](/wiki/University_of_Havana "University of Havana").[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Other tributes to *alma mater* include [Lorado Taft's](/wiki/Lorado_Taft "Lorado Taft") *[1929 sculpture](/wiki/Alma_Mater_(Illinois_sculpture) "Alma Mater (Illinois sculpture)")* at the [University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign](/wiki/University_of_Illinois_Urbana-Champaign "University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign") and [Cyrus Dallin's](/wiki/Cyrus_Edwin_Dallin "Cyrus Edwin Dallin") 1925 sculpture at the [Mary Institute](/wiki/Mary_Institute_and_St._Louis_Country_Day_School "Mary Institute and St. Louis Country Day School") in 1925, commissioned by [Washington University](/wiki/Washington_University_in_St._Louis "Washington University in St. Louis") supporters.

An altarpiece mural in Yale University's [Sterling Memorial Library](/wiki/Sterling_Memorial_Library "Sterling Memorial Library"), painted in 1932 by [Eugene Savage](/wiki/Eugene_Savage "Eugene Savage"), depicts the *Alma Mater* as a bearer of light and truth, standing in the midst of figures representing the arts and sciences.
